speaker: interviewer
text: It is November 30th, 1995. The name of the survivor is Edna Eizenstein. Her maiden name is Edna Dimmerman. I am Sharon Goszer-Tritt. We are in the city of Winnipeg. The country is Canada. And the language we will be speaking is English. My name is Sharon Goszer-Tritt. Today is Thursday, November 30th, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Edna Eizenstein. The city is Winnipeg. The province is Manitoba. And we are in Canada. The language of the interview will be English. Could you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Edna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eizenstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you like me to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you can spell it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. E-I-Z-E-N-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what your name was at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edna Dimmerman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can we spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hen-yah is spelled how?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. It's only the same like Edna.

speaker: interviewer
text: E-D-N-A.

speaker: interviewee
text: N-A, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was pronounced Hen-ya?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dimmerman-- D-I-M-M-E-R-M-A-N?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1932, second second.

speaker: interviewer
text: February 2, 1932.

speaker: interviewee
text: '32. Eizenstein your age today? 65.

speaker: interviewer
text: So today, February 2, 1932?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, '32. Yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So 63 years old?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your city of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kostopol.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I spell that for you? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: K-O-S

speaker: interviewee
text: T-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: T-O-P-O-L.

speaker: interviewee
text: L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: P-O-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewee
text: A-N-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about the city that you were born in. Tell me about Kostopol.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very nice city. It was a big-- not a very big one, smaller from-- from Rowne. Only it was a manufacture for wood. It's lots of people. What I remember, I just remember my family. We were-- I had a father and a mother. My father was-- his name was Yacov. My mother's name was Hannah. And I had a grandmother, Laiya. And my grandfather was Shai. And we were five children when we left the city. Then my mother thought we were going to return back. My grandparents didn't want to leave the house. And we thought we were going to leave just for one week, and we will come back. Then she left my oldest brother-- his name was [? Label-- ?] to look after the parents. And we went. We were walking to Kiev. When we came there, we couldn't return back. The German was already in our city. And my brother went-- thanks to that, what my brother went catching fish. He returned back. They told him, don't come. They took your grandparents. And that's was a place which would football-- what they used to play football. They used to take-- they took all the people what left. And they-- the [? bank ?] was making the [? board, ?] the holes. And a woman was alive, buried them alive. And then they killed the man. My brother saw that from a tree. He went up to in a tree, and he saw that. Then he didn't have choice. He left. He was running. And that-- that's all what I know for him. I never saw him. We didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so can I take you back a little bit to-- I'd like to find out more details about earlier on, before the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you started to tell me about your city, Kostopol. And it was about the size of Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I remember, I don't remember too much from the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any-- any buildings in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember buildings, where we used to live. I remember the Cheder where I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a Cheder. And it was the girls and the boys. It was the older one, the younger one was together. And they teached us how to daven. And you know, we used to-- at Jewish home, every child needed to know. And I-- I think I went for six month there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was six years or seven years. Six year. My brothers-- all my brother-- went to Cheder. The older sister of mine went to read in the Polish [INAUDIBLE]. She was the older one. And I don't remember too much. I can remember my neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, one neighbor was [? Pena ?] Abeloff. He had two boys. And he liked me a lot. You know? [LAUGHS] He used to call me [NON-ENGLISH]. He used to call me [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to ask me, open your both eyes. And I said, no, I'll open only one eye. I give you five pennies. I said, if I opened another one, would you give me 10 pennies for my brother, too, you know, for ice cream to buy. It was five pennies, you know? My grandfather, I remember. Tell me about your grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- my grandfather was a-- he made-- he made boots. Like not a shoes-- not assembly, like fixing shoes. He would making, for the police the-- the boots, the high. You know, in the old country, used to be police, high shoes. My-- my mother-- my grandfather mother was a very good cook. She baked very good. She cooked very good. And my older brother, we were scared very much. He was, like, the father for us. [LAUGHS] We were scared more for my brother like, from [INAUDIBLE]. You [? meet-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Our home was not far from a lake. We used to go always swimming there. And my father came looking for us. He gave us. But when my brother came, just with my-- his finger, we were scared, you know? I don't remember too much. Were there many Jewish people in Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lot. It was a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what languages were spoken in your home and in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our home was just Jewish. In the city, Polish and Ukrainian. Like there was a market. The Ukrainian, they didn't speak Polish. And in the houses were more-- more people-- more educated people. They spoke Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you mix at all with the non-Jewish people in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very, very kind. We never fight. We never. We had a close friend, I remember, was a neighbor, a gentile. We didn't have no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of things besides swimming did you do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: To play a doll, that's all I remember. And-- I don't remember much for fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any kinds of family celebrations, Jewish rituals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Pesach, we used to make the whole family and mine. I had an auntie, my mother's sister, was living not far from us. And always was a-- a Seder for 20, 25 people. And my father used to bring, always in the synagogue, a poor two people for-- for Pesach. I remember, every Saturday or Friday evening, because my father never came from the shul not to bring somebody to eat supper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a traditional Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's everybody was. Friday we used to bake. We couldn't wait for Friday. You know, we used to run from the Cheder to the house. And the smell was [LAUGHS] a long-- far from-- from the house. And we knew, already, every smell, which neighbor baked. [LAUGHS] And we used to know what mine-- my grandmother baked. And it was the good kugels and the milk put it in. Friday we couldn't light. We couldn't put it. Not even warm nothing. Only the oven was warm milk. And somebody-- for the children, it used to be chocolate milk. And everybody, that was us. Every Saturday, the mother used to bring to the kids to the bed the chocolate milk, with the-- with a beautiful bun. And every child liked something else. I liked with cinnamon, and somebody liked with chocolate. And that, was a very-- I can never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always with my grandfather and my father. Saturday, after we ate, the grown up didn't eat. But you can't eat when you go to the shul. But the children ate. And we used to take-- I used to take my grandfather, and my smaller brother used to take my father by the hand. Took the Tallits, and we used to go to the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the shul like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shul was Orthodoc. That's what mine parents went. The woman was upstairs. And it was with grapes, covered. We couldn't see the man. And the man was downstairs. You know? It was very nice. I remember the shul were very nice, how much I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to run around. Used to wait for the parents to come out. We used to play in-- in-- in buttons. [LAUGHS] And we used to run. Who's running faster. And we used to play a circle with girls and dancing. That was-- it's not like now. We never had toys. And we never had this [INAUDIBLE]. We used to make from little stones, and little things we used to make. And that was our toys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any favorite toys?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a favorite toy. And my sister made a doll. That was my favorite, favorite. I always went to sleep with her. And even to the Cheder, I needed to take my doll. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I called her Goldie. But [INAUDIBLE]. She made mine doll.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your sister's name is Goldie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you called the doll Goldie. Do you still have the doll? [LAUGHS] [SIGHS] Tell me more about family life. You said-- you told me a little bit about your father and your mother. Tell me about how they made a living, what the day-to-day experiences were like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my father was-- was very educated person. If somebody needed a lawyer in the city, they used to take my father. And they always won the case. In the winter, my father used to make furs. And in the summer, he used to make for the police the uniforms. We were-- my mother used to always go summer for two, three weeks, for a-- a place for resting. But she-- she was the sick person. We had a home-- a very nice house. We were comfortable in the house. My mother was a dressmaker before she got married. When she marry she-- she never worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she make you clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother? Sometimes. She used to do it for the older daughter, for my sister to make for me. But the most, she used to bring from Rowne. We had a big family in Rowne. My-- my father's coming from 12 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the family in Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had-- it's not [INAUDIBLE] first wife. They had two wives, he had. And they had 12, 12 boys and one daughter. And the whole was alive. Some was living in Kostopol. And some was-- the most was living in Rowne, and in Losice. But nobody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can I just clarify something? Your father had 12 brothers and one sister? Sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his mother was married two times.

speaker: interviewee
text: His father was married two times.

speaker: interviewer
text: His father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he had some brothers and sisters from the first?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I remember the 12-- the 12 boys is from the first wife. Only one daughter, the-- the younger one, baby, the daughter was from the second wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a close relationship with his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I-- I remembered the-- the brother who was living in-- in our city, yes. But I don't-- I don't remember. We used to go to take to Rowne, to go it's-- you know, it's only my mother used to go shopping. And mine older brother was cutting-- went up on a tree from cherries. And he fall down. And he was in pieces, broken. And they thought he's not going to survive. That time, my mother took him to Rowne. And he was one year in hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was [INAUDIBLE]. I was four years old when it's happened. Or I was three years old, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine grandmother. My mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandparents live close by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my parents were left to Israel with two children-- like the older brother, and my sister. And-- and in Israel, my-- another brother, Meir, he was born. And he was born blind. And my mother was worry. And the two kids, what they brought, they-- they got a sickness in the eyes, too. Then they told-- the doctor said, it's not going to help. The only thing, if you're going back, when the moment the [? ship ?] would go underwater from the Europe, the children will open their eyes. And they decided they came. And when they came, they didn't have a home. They came to  my grandparents. And my grandparents, build them-- they had property. And they build them a home attached to their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were very close to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very close. My grandmother used to bake. And my grandmother used to cook. And we were like one family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the evening, when you were a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a little girl, in the evening, after me ate-- that was Friday. After me ate, my father and my grandfather used to sit by the table and tell us stories from the Bible. And every child, he would asking to remember next week what we finished. And every child ask a question. And they used to answer the question. And in the middle of the week, my father used to read books for us, in the evening before we went to sleep. That was, for us, the best. I remember that was the best thing what could a child have, love from parents to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite bedtime story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any story I like. Any story what my father would told us, I liked it. I-- I didn't really have special to tell every time. I never wanted two times the same story. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you do with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother? I used to go with my mother to my mother's friend-- if I was good, if I was not punished. I used to go with my mother all the time. They used to wash clothes by the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the river? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember the name. I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very big river. Very big. And they used-- and the other side river was a big, big-- where they made flour. And that's made underwater, you know. It's the big wheel, and the wheel was running. And everybody from the farms who brought it to make flour. They used to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go with my mother to wash clothes, to help her, you know, to take it, what she washed it, to put it in another pail. I used to go help my grandfather to carry the water, you know? Nobody had water in their houses. Used to take from the big pail water at night. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather had a big wooden ones to put it on his shoulder, too. And we used to-- and I used to go and holding the hand. And I used to say, I brought water. [LAUGHS] It's nothing was special. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-- and once a year, we used to go camping. That was waiting, you know? We used to go and look. Mom, how long we have to wait [LAUGHS] to go again? I don't remember much. What kind of camp was it that you and your--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Betar camp. And they didn't take small children. Only it's-- if it was the bigger one, as the bigger one was looking afters. If I had my brother and my sister there, then my younger brother and me, we went in, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do at Betar camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp we used to-- the younger ones used to go and in to take berries and mushrooms, you know, under the leaves. We used to bring it. They used to tell us stories. They used to learn our songs. And the day was over, we used to make lots of fires. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember. Everything what I'm telling you, is some what my sister told me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any experiences that you remember really clearly at that Betar camp? Not something that somebody else told you, but something that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less, I remember. That's what I remember. We used to go there. And we used to sleep in-- in camps. And I always slept with my sister, which I was scared to sleep alone. I was scared for the bears. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was that time I went four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the camp in Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was far-- a little over the river. There was the camps. And there was lots of bears. You know? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Don't remember. I remember the neighbors. We-- I used to like to go into the neighbors. They had children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names, and how old were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one neighbor I know is Abeloff. He was a-- he was reading. He was a-- a printer for books and paper. That's was the only one in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I remember him. And on another side, how mine was the same thing, was a printer. I even don't remember the name, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they have children you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Abeloff, he had two boys. Yeah. One boy was the same age. And we were always together, you know? It was-- that time was different, playing and different toys. Today, you go out and you buy a toy. In that time, we needed to make myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father used to make us a little wagon for wood. And my brother was [INAUDIBLE] in. And I used to take him to the shul. And the wagon, used to put it under the steps, nobody to see. It was-- you can't go [? Shabbas, ?] you know? [LAUGHS] Were the Abeloffs a Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the name of-- names of the family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember only his name was [? Pena ?] Abeloff. And he had a son, was [? Label. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your friend, [? Label? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was the older one. The younger one, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me more about your brothers and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very close. We love each other very much. When-- to tell you how much we were so close, once upon a time, I broke a dish. And the dish was somebody gave my mother. It was an old one with beautiful flowers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother came in. And she-- she was mad. My brother, the younger one, came and said, mom, Edna didn't do it. I did it. You know? How much we were so close. If l like-- if my brother knew I like that piece of meat, he would take away from his dish and give it to me, you know. Nobody could touch me. Nobody could do something for me. If I came and I said that that boy gave me it, beat me up, don't say that. You know? We were very close. Tell me all your brothers names and your sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine older-- my oldest-- my sister is Goldie. My older brother is [? Label. ?] The-- the third brother, Meir. And then I came. I was Edna, and then Moshe. We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about what they did, what their life was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mine older brother was a little troublemaker, you know? Used to go all the trees, cutting the apples, and very-- very talent boy. And the-- the Meir was a very quiet boy, shy boy, very talent. In Israel, when he-- he was-- he was a-- he taught. Paper, he taught. That-- that he wrote a paper for the kibbutz. Moshe is very talent, too. Anything what you going in in his house is he made. The first time when I brought him here-- my sister, my brother to my daughter's wedding-- he liked so much my house. He liked the wood, and he liked the [? chandelier. ?] Two years later, I came to visit him. I couldn't believe it. He did over his house the same like mine. And he did it alone. He's very talent. He's right-- he was-- he finishes work, [? event ?] retiring very early. But the army didn't give him to go out. They call him back. And he's now is teaching in the school children, what are very low. You know, they are on top. They-- they don't want to be in the army. And he's a teacher. Like, [INAUDIBLE] hand, when he's going out, a boy, he's already good.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to bring you back now to when you were getting a little bit older-- I would imagine around six or seven years old. What were the first signs that you remember that-- that the war was approaching, that the war was going to begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody didn't believe it, we're going to have to leave-- leave the house to run away. We thought we're going go away someplace under the mountains, under the water, some, to be over the night and we would come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that there was a war going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father called us, all the kids, and told, it's a war. And nobody knows what going be, what the German would do to the Jewish people. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when he told you there was a war? What did that mean to a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared. I start crying. I was scared. Well, for moment we were playing, all we taking stuff, and taking food. And we were running totally for one night. We didn't think that we're going to never see again our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take anything other than food with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- how much food? We took a little bit bread. We took cheese. [INAUDIBLE] Anything what I remember-- jam. You know, my-- my grandmother used to make it alone, the jam. And wine, we took with us. And we took blankets. We took from the better stuff like, with us. And when we used to walk, we did that-- going, we walked. We're now [INAUDIBLE] to Tycyzn. We were there, Tyczyn, [INAUDIBLE]. And we heard it as the Germans are already in our city. We went, and we used to take off our better clothes and better stuff, to change for bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we-- we came to Kiev. And in-- in Kiev, was a train. And they said, only the woman with children going up in the train. The men would-- were not go up. And in that time, when we walked to the train, the airplanes came. And they started bomb. We didn't know fire from anyplace. And my father said, don't wait for Mom. And don't wait for-- for Dad. Run to hide yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became aware of the war through your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you started a journey.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through my father. Yeah. Always he told us, [INAUDIBLE] everyday, when we walk, he always said, be grave. They never-- if you get lost from us, don't stay in one place. When you see people, go to them and ask them, could you take me? I got lost. Tell your name. He-- he put every child, he put a name on-- on us. If somebody find us, we should know the smaller one, not the bigger one. And in Kiev, we got lost. And I never saw my parents again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I find a lady there with kids. And she asked me. I went to her. And she said, I have my own trouble alone. I can't look for another one. I have five kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stopped, and I-- I didn't know what to do. I was really disappointed, you know? Somebody refused to take me. I thought, I'm a child. I have the-- I have the right, somebody to help me. And at that time, I've-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We'll come back to that in a minute. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
